title: Blood on Leather
stainChemistry: Blood contains hemoglobin — an iron-bearing protein that bonds to leather through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. When fresh, the protein is water-soluble in cold conditions. Heat or time causes the protein chains to cross-link (denature), making the stain insoluble and far harder to remove. The iron component can leave a secondary rust-like discoloration after the protein is removed.
whyThisWorks: Cold water keeps the protein from denaturing and locking into the leather fiber. A digestant enzyme (professional) or surfactant (DIY) breaks down the protein chains into soluble fragments that rinse away. The key is treating before any heat exposure — heat permanently cross-links the protein.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to the stain and tamp gently with a spotting brush. Work from edge to center using COLD water only
2. Flush with cold water. Repeat NSD application if stain persists
3. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as a neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
4. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry completely — no heat
warnings: NO enzyme treatments on leather — enzymes digest protein fibers
COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein

steps: 1. Rinse immediately with COLD water. Blot with a clean white cloth — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup cold water. Apply to stain and blot
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat soap application until stain fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all soap residue
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer or any heat until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional who specializes in leather care. Do not attempt enzyme-based products at home — they will damage this fiber
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
NEVER use alcohol on blood — alcohol permanently sets protein stains
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first

neverDo: No acetone — strips finish and dye
No chlorine bleach — destroys leather protein
No alkaline cleaners — strips tanning agents
No excess water on aniline leather — causes permanent marks
fiberSensitivities: Pigmented leather has protective topcoat — more forgiving
Aniline leather has no topcoat — extremely stain-susceptible
Always condition after cleaning to prevent cracking

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is blood on leather, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
